Longroad Energy’s $2bn Sun Pond solar and storage project in Maricopa County, Arizona by Longroad Energy begins operations. The facility includes a 111MW-direct current (MWdc) solar component and an 85MW-alternating current (MWac)/340MW-hour storage component. Longroad Energy’s $2bn Sun Pond solar and storage project in Maricopa County, Arizona by Longroad Energy begins operations. The facility includes a 111MW-direct current (MWdc) solar component and an 85MW-alternating current (MWac)/340MW-hour storage compon...

Longroad Energy’s $2bn Sun Pond solar and storage project in Maricopa County, Arizona by Longroad Energy begins operations. The facility includes a 111MW-direct current (MWdc) solar component and an 85MW-alternating current (MWac)/340MW-hour storage component. It is part of the broader Longroad Sun Streams Complex which combines four projects totaling nearly 1.6GW of capacity. Furthermore, contracts via power purchase agreements have been secured with Ava Community Energy and San José Clean Energy for the facility’s output. Longroad Energy chief investment officer and co-founder Charles Spiliotis said: “With Sun Pond now operational, Longroad is pleased to be expanding access to renewable energy for customers in the greater Bay Area. “Sun Pond’s battery storage system also adds firm, flexible capacity – ensuring low-cost, clean power is available when the grid needs it most.” The project is expected to generate enough electricity annually to power nearly 35,000 US homes. The neighbouring state of Texas is also advancing its own major solar projects such as the 600MW Hornet solar plant in Swisher County, Texas. Since its completion in 2025, the plant generates enough electricity to power 160,000 homes in the state yearly. Scope of Implementation on the Sun Pond Solar Project in Arizona The Sun Pond solar project in Arizona includes various phases, I to III projects. It is managed by Longroad Energy in unincorporated Maricopa County, Arizona. It is involves constructing 80-acre solar energy facilities paired with battery energy storage. The implementation scope focuses on rezoning from rural-43 to industrial. It is developing photovoltaic solar arrays, and integrating with the grid near the Palo Verde Generating Station. Here is more information about the project: Location: East of S 351st Ave, North of Baseline Rd, South of S 350th Dr, West of W Fremont Rd, Arizona Facility Type: Solar energy generation with integrated battery energy storage systems (BESS). Land Use Change: Requesting a comprehensive plan amendment to change land use from rural densities to utilities. Zoning Action: Zone change with overlay from Rural-43 to IND-2 IUPD (Industrial Use Permit District). Developer: LSH Land Holdings, LLC The project, labeled under case numbers CPA2023003 and Z2023024, aims to improve energy capacity in the area near a major existing generating station. It is also expected reduce CO₂ emissions by more than 145,000t each year. This equates to removing nearly 34,000 petrol-powered vehicles from use. Construction was managed by McCarthy Building Companies, which employed more than 300 workers at its peak. Operations and also maintenance will be managed by NovaSource Power Services and Longroad Energy’s affiliate, Longroad Energy Services. Longroad Energy’s $2bn Sun Pond solar and storage project in Maricopa County, Arizona by Longroad Energy begins operations. Project Factsheet: Project Name: Sun Pond Solar and Storage Project Location: Maricopa County, Arizona, USA Estimated Investment Value: $2 Billion Project Type: Solar PV + Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) Timeline Construction Phase: Completed prior to 2026 2026: Project officially begins operations Operational Phase: Active and also supplying power under PPAs Site & Scale Solar Capacity: 111 MWdc Storage Capacity: 85 MWac / 340 MWh Part of: Longroad Sun Streams Complex (~1.6 GW total capacity) Land Area: Approx. 80 acres per phase Project Teams Developer: Longroad Energy Land Entity: LSH Land Holdings LLC EPC Contractor: McCarthy Building Companies O&M Providers: NovaSource Power Services, Longroad Energy Services Offtakers: Ava Community Energy, San José Clean Energy Infrastructure Scope Utility-scale photovoltaic solar arrays Integrated battery energy storage system (BESS) Also grid interconnection near Palo Verde Generating Station Multi-phase development (Phases I–III) Supporting infrastructure including inverters, substations, and also transmission links Strategic Objectives Expand renewable energy supply to California markets Provide firm and also dispatchable clean power Also reduce CO₂ emissions (~145,000 tonnes annually) Support grid reliability with flexible energy capacity Challenges Land rezoning and permitting (Rural-43 to industrial use) Grid integration and transmission coordination Also managing large-scale solar + storage operations Balancing supply with contracted demand obligations Current Status Project operational and delivering clean energy Also itegrated into Longroad’s broader Sun Streams portfolio Supplying electricity under long-term power purchase agreements Fully constructed with ongoing operations and also maintenance in place

APPENDIX II STOCK IDENTIFIERS

SECTION 1 CORPORATE PROFILE

1.1 Activities

Entergy Corporation is a major U.S.-based energy provider listed on the New York Stock Exchange, delivering reliable electric power to over 3 million customers across Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity through a diversified portfolio that includes nuclear, natural gas, coal, and renewable sources. In recent years, Entergy has prioritized growth in renewable energy segments, particularly solar and wind projects, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing demand for clean power and supportive regulatory environments. These initiatives align with the company's broader strategy to enhance grid reliability, reduce carbon emissions, and integrate advanced technologies for efficient energy delivery, positioning Entergy as a key player in the evolving utility sector. It is the NYSE's 4th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

Entergy Texas Orange County Advanced Power Station, Entergy Orange County Advanced Power Station, Entergy Corporation NYSE:ETR, and Entergy ETR achieved a significant milestone with the Entergy Texas first fire at the Orange County Advanced Power Station. This power station first fire signifies a crucial step in the Entergy power plant commissioning and Entergy plant startup. The first fire milestone Entergy underscores its commitment to enhancing electricity generation Texas through new power station Texas projects. The Entergy Texas new generation facility plays a pivotal role in energy infrastructure Texas by ensuring grid reliability Entergy and providing reliable energy Entergy. The advanced power station commissioning demonstrates Entergy's leadership in power generation technology. According to an Entergy press release Orange County, this Entergy project milestone is vital for power plant testing and commissioning. With the successful Entergy power station startup operations, the facility boosts Entergy generation capacity addition. The utility company Entergy news highlights meaningful progress towards Entergy construction completion milestone, paving the way for Entergy Texas energy projects.

SECTION 2 RECENT NEWS, PAST YEAR TOP 10 STORIES & PAST MONTH ECONOMIC NEWS

2.1 RECENT NEWS

Recent News 1 of 1, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 334 words

Thu Apr 23, 2026: EntergyTexas’ Orange County Advanced Power Station Reaches Key Development Milestone

EntergyTexas' Orange County Advanced Power Station (OCAPS) is one step closer to delivering reliable, affordable, and sustainable energy to support Southeast Texas' growing needs. The plant has successfully completed its 'first fire,' a major milestone toward commercial operation, on the 23rd of April 2026. This phase marks the first time fuel is introduced into the system and ignited, allowing crews to evaluate equipment performance and ensure all systems operate safely and in coordination.

Under the guidance of partners at Mitsubishi, Sargent & Lundy, and The Industrial Company (TIC), Entergy Texas CEO Eliecer Viamontes pressed the 'on' button, bringing the plant to life for the first time. Viamontes emphasized the importance of teamwork in achieving this milestone, stating, 'It was an honor to take part in this moment, but I know my role was the easiest part. All the credit belongs to the thousands of employees and vendor partners who have dedicated their skill, determination, and hundreds of thousands of hours to bring us to this point.'

Once fully operational, OCAPS, located in Orange, Texas, will generate about 1,215 megawatts of electricity — enough to power more than 230,000 homes. The facility is designed to support continued economic growth across Southeast Texas while reducing emissions through modern, efficient technology. Entergy Texas projects that its fuel efficiency will generate over $1 billion in customer savings over its lifetime.

With the first fire complete, teams will advance into additional testing as part of the commissioning process. OCAPS is scheduled to begin serving customers in late summer 2026. The completion of such infrastructure not only enhances the reliability of electricity supply but also aligns with Entergy Texas' commitment to sustainability and community support.

EntergyTexas serves approximately 538,000 customers in 27 counties and is a subsidiary of Entergy Corporation, which produces, transmits, and distributes electricity to around 3 million customers. The company is actively investing in cleaner energy generation, including modern natural gas, nuclear, and renewable sources, while ensuring that energy rates remain affordable for its customers.

2.2 PAST YEAR TOP 10 STORIES

Story 1 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 91 words

Meta's Hyperion Data Center: Louisiana PSC Approves $3.2 Billion Generation and Transmission Infrastructure

Entergy Louisiana has received approval from the Louisiana Public Service Commission for a $3.2 billion investment to build three natural gas power plants and a $550 million transmission line to support Meta's new Hyperion data center. The project, fast-tracked by regulators, will generate 2,262 megawatts. While Meta will cover initial infrastructure costs through a 15-year contract, all Entergy customers will bear ongoing fuel, operational, and transmission costs. The project is expected to generate $178 million in annual profits for Entergy but has faced opposition over costs, environmental impact, and grid reliability.

Story 2 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 84 words

Amazon's $3 Billion Warren County Data Center Investment: Largest Private Investment in County History

Amazon announced a $3 billion investment to build a new data center campus in Warren County, Mississippi, the largest private investment in the county's history. The project is expected to create around 200 direct full-time jobs and support over 300 additional community positions. Entergy Mississippi's commitment to providing reliable power was a key factor in the decision. The investment is anticipated to generate significant tax revenue and is supported by local workforce development partnerships, reinforcing Mississippi's position as a growing hub for AI infrastructure.

Story 3 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 78 words

Q3 2025 Financial Results: Narrowed Guidance and Strategic Regulatory Milestone Execution

Entergy reported third-quarter 2025 earnings of $1.53 per share, a slight year-over-year increase, and narrowed its full-year adjusted EPS guidance to $3.85-$3.95. The quarter was marked by significant regulatory approvals for major projects, including infrastructure for Meta's data center in Louisiana and two power stations in Texas. Despite higher operating costs, the company saw year-to-date earnings improve, partly due to favorable weather. The results highlight Entergy's progress in executing its capital-intensive strategy focused on meeting data center demand.

Story 4 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 79 words

Legend and Lone Star Power Stations: PUCT Approves 1,200 MW Combined Generation Capacity Supporting Southeast Texas Growth

The Public Utility Commission of Texas approved Entergy Texas's plan to build the Legend and Lone Star power stations, two natural gas plants with a combined capacity of 1,200 megawatts. The total investment is nearly $2.4 billion and is expected to generate $2.8 billion in regional economic activity and over 9,000 construction jobs. The new facilities are designed to meet rising electricity demand from rapid population and economic growth in Southeast Texas, improve grid reliability, and lower electricity costs.

Story 5 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 79 words

Jefferson Power Station: Arkansas Investment Anchors State Electricity Infrastructure Modernization

Entergy Arkansas announced a $1.6 billion investment to construct the Jefferson Power Station, a 754-megawatt natural gas facility in Jefferson County, scheduled for operation in 2029. The plant, which will be 40% more efficient than legacy units, is expected to generate $2.9 billion in economic activity during construction. It will provide reliable baseload power, support industrial growth, and integrate with renewable projects. The investment is a key part of modernizing Arkansas's energy infrastructure and retiring less efficient coal-fired generation.

Story 6 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 79 words

Google's Cypress Solar Project: 600 MW Solar-Plus-350 MW Battery Storage Investment for Arkansas Data Center

Entergy Arkansas plans to develop a 600-megawatt solar facility with a 350-megawatt battery storage system to power Google's new data centers in the state. Google will cover the full cost of the project, ensuring no financial burden falls on existing Entergy customers. The project is expected to deliver $1.1 billion in net benefits to Arkansas ratepayers over its lifetime. This partnership supports Google's renewable energy goals while providing the reliable power needed for its AI and cloud computing operations.

Story 7 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 61 words

Dividend Increase: Reinforced Shareholder Return Policy Reflects Financial Confidence

Entergy's board of directors approved a $0.04 dividend increase, raising the quarterly payout to $0.64 per share. This marks the 11th consecutive year of dividend increases for the company, which has paid dividends continuously since 1988. The move signals management's confidence in its financial stability, earnings growth, and ability to fund its large-scale capital investment plan while delivering consistent shareholder returns.

Story 8 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 73 words

SETEX Transmission Project: PUCT Approves 145-Mile 500-kV Line and $200 Million Texas Energy Fund Grant

Entergy Texas received approval from the Public Utility Commission of Texas for the $1.4 billion SETEX project, a 145-mile, 500-kilovolt transmission line designed to enhance grid reliability in Southeast Texas. The project was identified as critical by the Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO). Concurrently, Entergy Texas was awarded a $200 million grant from the Texas Energy Fund to support grid hardening and resilience initiatives against extreme weather, further strengthening the region's electrical infrastructure.

Story 9 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 69 words

Orange County Advanced Power Station: Final Construction Phase Nearing Summer 2026 Operational Commencement

Entergy Texas's Orange County Advanced Power Station, a 1,215-megawatt combined-cycle plant, is in its final construction phase and is expected to become operational in summer 2026. The facility features modern, hydrogen-capable technology and is projected to save customers over $100 million in fuel costs in its first year. The plant is a key part of Entergy's strategy to meet growing demand in Southeast Texas with efficient and resilient generation.

Story 10 of 10, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 74 words

$40 Billion Capital Plan and Extended Earnings Guidance: Strategic Infrastructure Investment Supporting Growth and Shareholder Returns

Entergy has increased its four-year capital investment plan to $40 billion through 2028 to fund generation, transmission, and renewable energy projects. The company also raised its future earnings per share guidance for 2027 and 2028, projecting over 8% compound annual growth. This expanded plan reflects management's confidence in securing regulatory approvals and capitalizing on growth from data centers and other industrial customers, balanced with the need to raise significant capital to fund the initiatives.

2.3 ECONOMIC NEWS

ECO NEWS 1 of 2, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 226 words

Apr 22, 2026: Anterix Executes $771,000 Benton PUD Private Wireless Deal, Reinforcing Utility Private-LTE Ecosystem Relevant to Entergy's Gulf Coast Resilience Strategy

On April 21, 2026, Anterix Inc., through PDV Spectrum Holding Company, LLC, signed a spectrum license sale agreement with Public Utility District No. 1 of Benton County for a 10 MHz 900 MHz broadband license covering Washington state. According to industry reports, the agreement carries a total contract value of $771,000, with payments tied to FCC license issuance and assignment.

Market data indicates the utility-owned network will cover a 927-square-mile territory and more than 58,000 customers, marking the first private wireless broadband deployment among publicly owned utilities in the Pacific Northwest. The deployment expands the national base of 900 MHz private-LTE utility networks.

Impact on Entergy: ETR

Following this development, Entergy, which has deployed Anterix 900 MHz spectrum across portions of its service territory, gains additional industry validation supporting its ongoing private LTE rollout. Operational metrics indicate Entergy serves approximately 3 million utility customers across Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas, a region heavily exposed to hurricanes and severe weather.

Analysis shows the Benton PUD agreement expands the pool of operating utility deployments, reinforcing technology maturity and supply-chain scale for devices, radios, and core infrastructure. Additional utility adoption supports shared operational learnings relevant to storm hardening and restoration, priorities given Entergy's Gulf Coast exposure and industrial load growth.

ECO NEWS 2 of 2, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 272 words

Apr 13, 2026: Oregon Appeals Court Reverses PacifiCorp Wildfire Verdict, Creating Favorable Legal Precedent for Entergy Corp Amid Gulf Coast Storm Litigation Risks

On April 8, 2026, the Oregon Court of Appeals ruled in favor of Berkshire Hathaway Energy's PacifiCorp subsidiary in the James class-action case tied to Oregon's 2020 Labor Day wildfires. A three-judge appellate panel found that the trial court erred in instructing the jury that it could assume evidence presented at trial regarding four different wildfires applied to all class members. The ruling reverses and remands the case back to the trial court.

According to financial estimates, Berkshire has projected the cost could reach tens of billions of dollars, including $1.1 billion awarded in a series of already-completed trials. The decision could pause more than 160 damages trials scheduled through 2027. Separately, PacifiCorp has agreed to pay over $2 billion to settle claims, including $575 million to the federal government. Plaintiffs' counsel has called the ruling a procedural setback and may appeal to the Oregon Supreme Court.

Impact on Entergy Corp: ETR

Following this news, Entergy Corp faces an improved precedent environment for defending against natural disaster-related class-action claims. Entergy serves approximately 3 million customers across Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas, a region regularly impacted by hurricanes, flooding, and severe storms.

The company has previously faced litigation related to power restoration timelines and infrastructure damage following major hurricanes. The court's ruling that evidence specific to individual incidents cannot be applied across an entire class of geographically dispersed plaintiffs provides a procedural defense framework directly applicable to Entergy's exposure in storm-related litigation, where damages vary substantially by location, storm severity, and infrastructure conditions.

urn:contentItem:6JJP-2CB3-SHCH-60T1-00000-00Promising Energy Stocks To Add to Your Watchlist - May 10th2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:01:43ZLexisNexisPromising Energy Stocks To Add to Your Watchlist - May 10th

Tesla, IREN, Bloom Energy, ExxonMobil, Coterra Energy, Chevron, and GE Vernova are the seven Energy stocks to watch today, according to MarketBeat's stock screener tool. Energy stocks are shares of companies involved in producing, exploring, refining, transporting, or distributing energy resources such as oil, natural gas, and electricity. For stock market investors, the term usually refers to companies in sectors like oil and gas, utilities, and renewable energy that can offer returns tied to energy prices, demand, and regulatory changes. These companies had the highest dollar trading volume of any Energy stocks within the last several days.

Tesla TSLA

Tesla, Inc. designs, develops, manufactures, leases, and sells electric vehicles, and energy generation and storage systems in the United States, China, and internationally. The company operates in two segments, Automotive, and Energy Generation and Storage. The Automotive segment offers electric vehicles, as well as sells automotive regulatory credits; and non-warranty after-sales vehicle, used vehicles, body shop and parts, supercharging, retail merchandise, and vehicle insurance services.

Read Our Latest Research Report on TSLA

IREN IREN

IREN Limited, formerly known as Iris Energy Limited, owns and operates bitcoin mining data centers. The company was incorporated in 2018 and is headquartered in Sydney, Australia.

Read Our Latest Research Report on IREN

Bloom Energy BE

Bloom Energy Corporation designs, manufactures, sells, and installs solid-oxide fuel cell systems for on-site power generation in the United States and internationally. The company offers Bloom Energy Server, a solid oxide technology that converts fuel, such as natural gas, biogas, hydrogen, or a blend of these fuels into electricity through an electrochemical process without combustion.

Read Our Latest Research Report on BE

ExxonMobil XOM

ExxonMobil Corporation engages in the exploration and production of crude oil and natural gas in the United States and internationally. It operates through Upstream, Energy Products, Chemical Products, and Specialty Products segments. The Upstream segment explores for and produces crude oil and natural gas.

Read Our Latest Research Report on XOM

Coterra Energy CTRA

Coterra Energy Inc., an independent oil and gas company, engages in the development, exploration, and production of oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids in the United States. The company's properties include the Marcellus Shale with approximately 186,000 net acres in the dry gas window of the play located in Susquehanna County, Pennsylvania; Permian Basin properties with approximately 296,000 net acres located in westTexas and southeast New Mexico; and Anadarko Basin properties with approximately 182,000 net acres located in Oklahoma.

Read Our Latest Research Report on CTRA

ChevronCVX

Chevron Corporation, through its subsidiaries, engages in the integrated energy and chemicals operations in the United States and internationally. The company operates in two segments, Upstream and Downstream. The Upstream segment is involved in the exploration, development, production, and transportation of crude oil and natural gas; processing, liquefaction, transportation, and regasification of liquefied natural gas; transportation of crude oil through pipelines; transportation, storage, and marketing of natural gas; and carbon capture and storage, as well as a gas-to-liquids plant.

Read Our Latest Research Report on CVX

GE Vernova GEV

GE Vernova LLC, an energy business company, generates electricity. It operates under three segments: Power, Wind, and Electrification. The Power segments generates and sells electricity through hydro, gas, nuclear, and steam power. Wind segment engages in the manufacturing and sale of wind turbine blades; and Electrification segment provides grid solutions, power conversion, solar, and storage solutions.

Read Our Latest Research Report on GEV

value.Title: USA Rare Earth Inc. developing projects in Stillwater , West Texas
value.Overview: The Lawton Constitution (Oklahoma), NEWS, 175words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGH-KDP3-SD74-82R4-00000-00USA Rare Earth Inc. developing projects in Stillwater, West Texas2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:33ZLexisNexisNEWSUSA Rare Earth Inc. developing projects in Stillwater, West TexasStaff Report

STILLWATER Oklahoma's USA Rare Earth, a mining and manufacturing company focused on developing a fully integrated, domestic, "mine-to-magnet" supply chain for rare earth elements, announced it is developing the Round Top Mountain heavy rare-earths and critical minerals project in West Texas, alongside a high-powered magnet manufacturing facility in Stillwater.

The company said its aim is to break dependence on foreign sources (specifically China) by mining heavy rare earths in Texas and producing permanent magnets for defense, electric vehicles and renewable energy. The company plans to be fully operational by 2027-2028.

The company said the Trump administration took an equity stake through the Commerce Department in January 2026 the first such government investment in heavy rare earths.

The Round Top mining and processing project is targeted for opening by end of 2028 and is about two years ahead of prior projections. USA Rare Earth's acquisition of U.K.-based Less Common Metals provides a processing and metal-making hub outside China.

value.Title: Endesa Secures LNG Carrier 'Adriano Knutsen' for Long-term Charter
value.Overview: Global Round Up - ADRs and Depository Receipts, 616words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-Y103-S8YC-S336-00000-00Endesa Secures LNG Carrier 'Adriano Knutsen' for Long-term Charter2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisEndesa Secures LNG Carrier 'Adriano Knutsen' for Long-term Charter

DAILY ADR AND DEPOSITORY RECEIPT REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

06 May 2026 10:00 EDT

Endesa will charter the LNG carrier 'Adriano Knutsen' from Knutsen OAS Shipping for a period starting next summer. This announcement comes on the 5th of May, 2026, marking a significant shift in Endesa's LNG procurement strategy, as the company transitions to long-term time charter contracts for liquefied natural gas (LNG) imports.

This decision marks the first time Endesa has opted for such a charter arrangement, having previously relied on CIF purchase contracts. In 2014, the company secured a substantial agreement to purchase two million cubic meters of natural gas annually from Cheniere Energy’s Corpus Christi project in Texas for a 20-year term.

The 'Adriano Knutsen' is a third-generation LNG carrier, measuring 300 meters in length and 48 meters in width, with a capacity of transporting 180,000 cubic meters of LNG. Equipped with a total reliquefaction system onboard, the carrier will enable the reuse of evaporated gas, enhancing operational efficiency. The charter contract is valued at approximately $25 million annually, while the construction cost of the vessel is estimated at $185 million.

Construction of the 'Adriano Knutsen' is progressing well at Hyundai Heavy Industries in South Korea. Sea and load tests are scheduled to commence in April, with the vessel expected to become operational by July. Each year, the LNG carrier is projected to facilitate up to twelve transatlantic or six transpacific trips from the Corpus Christi terminal, operating at a contractual speed of 19.5 knots.

Following the 'Adriano Knutsen', a second vessel named 'Trajano Knutsen' is set to follow. Its construction has just begun at the same shipyard, with an anticipated delivery in mid-2020. The strategic chartering of the 'Adriano Knutsen' aims to partially meet the maritime transportation needs for natural gas for both Endesa and its parent company, Enel.

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1 ENDESA ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 Endesa Activities

Activities

Endesa, a leading Spanish energy company and subsidiary of Enel Group, operates primarily in the electricity sector across Spain and Portugal. Its core business activities encompass electricity generation from a diverse mix of sources, including renewables like wind and solar, as well as thermal and hydroelectric power; distribution and supply of electricity and natural gas; and customer-focused services such as energy efficiency solutions. In recent years, Endesa's fastest-growing segments have been its renewable energy initiatives, driven by significant investments in wind and solar projects to meet rising demand for sustainable power and comply with EU decarbonization targets. Additionally, the company is expanding in electric mobility and smart grid technologies, positioning itself as a key player in the transition to a low-carbon economy while maintaining a strong focus on operational efficiency and customer service. It is the International Depository Receipt (IDR) market's 4th largest Electrical company by market capitalisation.

Eric L. Muñoz brings more than a decade of experience to Womble Bond 's finance, bankruptcy...

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHF-07D3-SG04-B05G-00000-00Womble Bond Adds Akin Gump Finance Pro In Houston2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisNEWSWomble Bond Adds Akin Gump Finance Pro In HoustonLynn LaRowe

Womble Bond Dickinson has strengthened its debt financing capabilities in the energy sector with the hiring of a Houston-based partner who came aboard from Akin Gump Strauss Hauer & Feld LLP. Eric L. Muñoz brings more than a decade of experience to Womble Bond's finance, bankruptcy...

Womble Bond Dickinson has strengthened its debt financing capabilities in the energy sector with the hiring of a Houston-based partner who came aboard from Akin Gump Strauss Hauer & Feld LLP. Eric L. Muñoz brings more than a decade of experience to Womble ...

Womble Bond Dickinson has strengthened its debt financing capabilities in the energy sector with the hiring of a Houston-based partner who came aboard from Akin Gump Strauss Hauer & Feld LLP. Eric L. Muñoz brings more than a decade of experience to Womble Bond's finance, bankruptcy and restructuring practice, Womble Bond said Monday in a statement. Muñoz represents borrowers, issuers and investors in complex financing transactions. His experience includes syndicated and private credit facilities, high-yield and bridge financings, acquisition financings, project finance and derivative transactions, the firm said. Muñoz said Wednesday in an email to Law360 Pulse, "I was initially attracted to Womble Bond Dickinson because of the firm's commitment to growing its energy practice and its presence in Houston." "I became excited about the opportunity to join Womble Bond Dickinson after meeting a number of folks across different practice groups, not only because of their expertise and experience, but their commitment to continuing to build a high-quality platform focused on client success," Muñoz said. "In addition, the opportunities to expand my practice with a firm that is not only a full-service firm but also has such a significant presence, particularly in the U.S., was also very attractive. I believe that being part of a growing team with full-service capabilities and dedication to helping clients navigate complex issues is vital in today's dynamic legal environment, and I am very much looking forward to this next chapter in my career." Muñoz advises energy companies, developers and financial institutions on structuring and executing financings, the firm said. His work centers on oil and gas, renewable energy and emerging technologies, particularly with upstream and midstream companies, oilfield services providers and energy developers. Taber Cathcart, co-leader of Womble Bond's finance, bankruptcy and restructuring practice group, said in a statement, "Clients are increasingly navigating a more complex financing environment where bank capital, private credit, and structured solutions all play a role." "Eric's experience across those markets, particularly in energy and borrower-side transactions, fits directly with the needs we're seeing from clients across the sector," Cathcart said. Muñoz practiced with Akin for more than 18 years, the bulk of his legal career, his LinkedIn profile shows. After earning his law degree from the University of Houston Law Center, Muñoz served for a year as a law clerk in the Southern Districtof Texas. He holds a bachelor's degree from Stanford University. Muñoz serves on the board of the Hispanic Bar Association of Houston and on the board of Texas Appleseed, a nonprofit organization that advocates at the state and local level for fair, just and equitable laws, according to the organization's website. Womble Bond employs over 1,300 lawyers across 40 locations in the U.S. and the U.K. --Editing by Rich Mills.

value.Title: Meta and DESRI sign 850 MW clean power PPAs in US
value.Overview: Greentechlead.com, 195words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-7DX3-SGGF-42WS-00000-00Meta and DESRI sign 850 MW clean power PPAs in US2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:01:42ZLexisNexisMeta and DESRI sign 850 MW clean power PPAs in USNew Delhi


May 14 --

DESRI and Meta have signed 850 MW of solar and battery storage power purchase agreements (PPAs) in US. The agreement includes 500 MW in Oklahoma, 200 MW in Texas and 150 MW in Mississippi. With these agreements, the total contracted solar and battery storage capacity between the companies has increased to approximately 2,575 MW in US.



Previously contracted projects between the companies include 150 MW in Texas, 300 MW in Utah and 100 MW in New Mexico. DESRI expects roughly 1,110 MW of these contracted renewable energy projects to begin construction in 2026. Each project is expected to generate hundreds of construction-related jobs while supporting local economic development over the course of its operational lifespan.

In January 2026, Meta and Origis Energy entered into a long-term power purchase agreement for the Greyhound A solar project located in West Odessa, Texas. The project has a capacity of 303 MWdc, equivalent to 240 MWac, and is scheduled to reach commercial operations by mid-2026. Greyhound A forms part of a 1 GW broader energy infrastructure complex with a fully subscribed solar portfolio being developed by Origis Energy in west Texas.

value.Title: Leeward Renewable Energy Operations Announces the Posting of Q4 2025 Financial Results and May 12 Investor Call
value.Overview: Business Wire, 179words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-M2K3-SG5M-90X8-00000-00Leeward Renewable Energy Operations Announces the Posting of Q4 2025 Financial Results and May 12 Investor Call2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisLeeward Renewable Energy Operations Announces the Posting of Q4 2025 Financial Results and May 12 Investor CallDALLAS

Leeward Renewable Energy Operations, LLC ("LREO") today announced that it has posted key operating and financial results for the fourth quarter of 2025 to its secure investor relations site.

An investor call will be held on May 12, from 1:00 to 2:00 p.m. CST. Details to access the call have been posted to LREO's secure investor relations site.

For information on how to access the site, visit https://www.lreus.com/partner-with-us/investors/ or contact Investor Relations at bond.holders@leewardenergy.com .

About Leeward Renewable Energy

Founded in Texas in 2003, LRE (Leeward Renewable Energy) owns and operates 30+ wind, solar, and energy storage projects totaling over 4 GW nationwide. LRE continues to help meet increased U.S. energy demand through its robust pipeline of new projects every year. We prioritize domestic jobs, local community partnerships, and protecting the land in our long-term approach to meet American energy needs. To learn more, visit www.lreus.com .

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260507953496/en/



CONTACT: For more information: Otilia Ayats-Mas otilia.ayatsmas@leewardenergy.com

value.Title: Tesla's Energy Division Surpasses Auto Sales in Australia
value.Overview: News Bites US Markets, 708words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-VSX3-S55N-02JH-00000-00Tesla's Energy Division Surpasses Auto Sales in Australia2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisTesla's Energy Division Surpasses Auto Sales in Australia

AMERICAN DAILY STOCK REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

04 May 2026 08:45 EDT

Tesla's energy storage division has officially eclipsed its automotive segment as the primary revenue driver in Australia. Financial reports for the year ending 31st December 2025 indicate a notable shift in the company's earnings dynamics, with total revenue decreasing by 11% to AUD 4.5 billion and net profit after tax falling 19% to AUD 52.7 million.

The energy generation and storage products, which include the residential Powerwall and industrial-scale Megapacks, generated an impressive AUD 2.5 billion, surpassing the AUD 1.92 billion from vehicle sales. This internal rebalancing comes against a backdrop of increasing competition in the local electric vehicle (EV) market, where Tesla's annual sales declined by 25%, amounting to 28,856 units sold. In stark contrast, Chinese rival BYD achieved 52,000 deliveries, solidifying its position as a market leader.

Despite the ongoing geopolitical tensions, particularly the conflict in Iran that has driven petrol prices to record highs and renewed interest in electrification, Tesla's market position has faced challenges. By March, BYD had even secured the third-place spot across all fuel types in Australia, leaving Tesla trailing in 13th place. Such developments highlight the fierce competition Tesla faces in the Australian EV landscape.

Amid this automotive downturn, Tesla has managed to leverage the federal government's new vehicle efficiency scheme, securing a AUD 3 million regulatory credit for its zero-emission fleet. While the company's directors have opted not to comment further on the situation, analysts are suggesting that Tesla's dual-threat strategy—balancing its energy and automotive divisions—may be acting as a crucial buffer against the prevailing market challenges.

As Tesla continues to navigate these complexities in Australia, the divergence between its energy and automotive sectors may provide insights into the company's long-term strategy and adaptability in the face of fierce competition and evolving market dynamics.

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1 TESLA ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 Tesla Activities

Activities

Tesla, Inc., a leading American electric vehicle and clean energy company headquartered in Austin, Texas, and listed on the Nasdaq, focuses on designing, manufacturing, and selling innovative electric cars, energy storage systems, and solar products. Its core business revolves around accelerating the global transition to sustainable energy, with flagship electric vehicles like the Model 3 and Model Y dominating the market through advanced battery technology and performance features. The fastest-growing segments include energy storage solutions, such as the Powerwall and Megapack, which are increasingly adopted for residential, commercial, and utility-scale applications to support renewable energy integration and grid stability. Additionally, Tesla's autonomous driving technology and artificial intelligence capabilities, embedded in vehicles and expanded through its Full Self-Driving software, are experiencing rapid expansion, driven by rising demand for smart mobility solutions. This diversification, coupled with global manufacturing expansions in China and Germany, positions Tesla as a key player in the evolving sustainable technology landscape. It is the NASDAQ's largest Auto company by market capitalisation.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJT-B2J3-RVXF-S537-00000-00United States set record energy production in 20252026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:01:42ZLexisNexisNEWSUnited States set record energy production in 2025Umer Jamshaid

WASHINGTON, (UrduPoint / Pakistan Point News / WAM - 13th May, 2026) Total energy production in the United States increased to a new record of 107 quadrillion British thermal units (quads) in 2025, a 3.4% increase from...

WASHINGTON, (UrduPoint / Pakistan Point News / WAM - 13th May, 2026) Total energy production in the United States increased to a new record of 107 quadrillion British thermal units (quads) in 2025, a 3.4% increase from the previous record set in 2024, acco...

WASHINGTON, (UrduPoint / Pakistan Point News / WAM - 13th May, 2026) Total energy production in the United States increased to a new record of 107 quadrillion British thermal units (quads) in 2025, a 3.4% increase from the previous record set in 2024, according to new data in Monthly Energy Review released by the US Energy Information Administration. Total production was driven by record-high production in natural gas, crude oil, natural gas plant liquids (NGPLs), and renewables. This was the fourth consecutive year in which the United States set a record for total energy production, the report noted. Dry natural gas production grew more than 4% from 2024 to a record-high 39 trillion cubic feet in 2025, with most of the growth occurring in the Appalachia, Permian, and Haynesville regions. Natural gas has been the largest source of US domestic energy production since 2011, and the United States has been the largest natural gas producer in the world since 2011. Crude oil production also set a record 13.6 million barrels per day in 2025 and grew by 3%, or 350,000 barrels per day, compared with the previous record set in 2024. Most of that growth occurred in the Permian region of western Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Crude oil accounted for 26% of domestic energy production, and the United States remained the largest crude oil producer in the world. Production of NGPLs, which are hydrocarbons separated as liquids during natural gas processing, grew 7% to a record 4 trillion cubic feet in 2025 compared with the previous record set in 2024. In 2025, NGPLs accounted for 9% of domestic energy production. NGPL production has grown every year since 2005 as natural gas production and processing have increased. Renewable energy production grew by 3% from 2024 to a new record, the fifth consecutive year of growth. Solar and wind both set records for energy production as new generators came online. Geothermal, hydroelectric, and wood and waste energy production remained steady from 2024 to 2025. Biofuels production, which had grown in four consecutive years, declined slightly. Coal accounted for 10% of domestic energy production in 2025. It increased by 4% from 2024 levels to 533 million short tons in 2025 after two years of declining production.

value.Title: PETAN boss advocates local capacity to secure Africa ’s energy future
value.Overview: CHAIRMAN, Petroleum Technology Association of Nigeria, PETAN, Mr Wole Ogunsanya, has urged African countries to strengthen local capacity and harness natural resources to secure their energy future amid global shifts Ogunsanya said this in an interview with newsmen on the sidelines of the Offshore Technology Conference ( OTC ) 2026 in Houston , Texas , U.S....

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHJ-TPB3-RX9F-C11P-00000-00PETAN boss advocates local capacity to secure Africa’s energy future2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisNEWSPETAN boss advocates local capacity to secure Africa’s energy future

CHAIRMAN, Petroleum Technology Association of Nigeria, PETAN, Mr Wole Ogunsanya, has urged African countries to strengthen local capacity and harness natural resources to secure their energy future amid global shifts Ogunsanya said this in an interview with newsmen on the sidelines of the Offshore Technology Conference (OTC) 2026 in Houston, Texas, U.S....

CHAIRMAN, Petroleum Technology Association of Nigeria, PETAN, Mr Wole Ogunsanya, has urged African countries to strengthen local capacity and harness natural resources to secure their energy future amid global shifts Ogunsanya said this in an interview wit...

CHAIRMAN, Petroleum Technology Association of Nigeria, PETAN, Mr Wole Ogunsanya, has urged African countries to strengthen local capacity and harness natural resources to secure their energy future amid global shifts Ogunsanya said this in an interview with newsmen on the sidelines of the Offshore Technology Conference (OTC) 2026 in Houston, Texas, U.S. Speaking on the conference theme, “Africa’s Energy Transformation: Scaling Investments, Technology and Local Capacity for Sustainable Growth”, Ogunsanya said Africa must take urgent steps to secure its energy future. “The world is changing rapidly. Countries are becoming more regional in their economic and energy priorities. Energy security is now a major global concern,” he said. Ogunsanya pointed to major investments in shale gas in the U.S., Europe’s renewable energy push and China’s dominance in electric vehicle and battery technology as indicators of the changing global energy landscape. According to him, Africa must leverage its abundant oil and gas resources to drive industrialisation, economic growth and energy independence. “Africa cannot afford to be left behind. “We may not yet dominate battery technology, but we have enormous oil and gas resources that the world still needs,” he said. Ogunsanya said Africa holds more than 120 billion barrels of oil reserves and vast gas deposits, while Nigeria alone has over 200 trillion cubic feet of gas reserves. “The resources are there. The challenge is how Africa can harness them for development,” he said. He linked low energy consumption across Africa to poverty, weak industrial growth and lower life expectancy. “People with access to energy preserve food better, process agricultural products, store medicine properly and ultimately live longer,” he said. Ogunsanya stressed that closing Africa’s energy gap was critical to improving living standards and creating opportunities for millions of young people across the continent. He said PETAN had continued to promote indigenous participation and regional collaboration through the African Local Content Association (ALCA). According to him, Nigerian firms now possess the technical expertise and operational experience needed to support energy projects across Africa. “The capacity Nigeria has today in oil and gas is greater than that of many African countries combined. “We have a responsibility to support our African brothers and help transfer knowledge faster across the continent,” he said. Ogunsanya credited Nigeria’s local content policy and the Nigerian Oil and Gas Industry Content Development Act 2010 for strengthening indigenous capacity in the sector. He added that many successful Nigerian energy companies emerged from decades of experience acquired while working with multinational firms. “What took Nigeria nearly 70 years to learn can now be transferred to other African countries within 10 to 15 years through collaboration,” he said. Ogunsanya also welcomed plans for the launch of the African Energy Bank in July 2026, describing it as a significant step toward financing indigenous energy development. “We have the people, the resources and the market. “What we need now is financing, collaboration and the political will to build Africa for Africans,” he said. He reiterated PETAN’s commitment to promoting policies and partnerships that support local participation, technology transfer and sustainable energy growth across Africa. (NAN) A.I May 7, 2026

urn:contentItem:6JH9-WB13-S0BG-B2P5-00000-00Top Oil Stocks Worth Watching - May 5th2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:01:43ZLexisNexisTop Oil Stocks Worth Watching - May 5th

The Crude Reality: Pullback Creates High-Conviction Opportunity in Chevron

ExxonMobil, Diamondback Energy, and Shell are the three Oil stocks to watch today, according to MarketBeat's stock screener tool. Oil stocks are shares of publicly traded companies whose principal businesses involve the petroleum industry-including exploration and production upstream, refining and marketing downstream, and oilfield services and equipment. Investors buy oil stocks to gain exposure to crude-oil prices and energy demand, typically accepting higher cyclicality, commodity-price risk, and geopolitical and regulatory sensitivity, while some oil companies also provide dividend income. These companies had the highest dollar trading volume of any Oil stocks within the last several days.

ExxonMobil XOM

ExxonMobil Corporation engages in the exploration and production of crude oil and natural gas in the United States and internationally. It operates through Upstream, Energy Products, Chemical Products, and Specialty Products segments. The Upstream segment explores for and produces crude oil and natural gas.

Strait to Safety: How to Hedge Oil Volatility in the Crude Awakening

Read Our Latest Research Report on XOM

Diamondback Energy FANG

Diamondback Energy, Inc., an independent oil and natural gas company, acquires, develops, explores, and exploits unconventional, onshore oil and natural gas reserves in the Permian Basin in West Texas. It focuses on the development of the Spraberry and Wolfcamp formations of the Midland basin; and the Wolfcamp and Bone Spring formations of the Delaware basin, which are part of the Permian Basin in West Texas and New Mexico.

The $100 Barrel Is Back: Trading The Hormuz Havoc

Read Our Latest Research Report on FANG

Shell SHEL

Shell plc operates as an energy and petrochemical company Europe, Asia, Oceania, Africa, the United States, and Rest of the Americas. The company operates through Integrated Gas, Upstream, Marketing, Chemicals and Products, and Renewables and Energy Solutions segments. It explores for and extracts crude oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids; markets and transports oil and gas; produces gas-to-liquids fuels and other products; and operates upstream and midstream infrastructure to deliver gas to market.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJX-FBB3-RWNR-G0N1-00000-00Adam Schiff, local representatives condemn Sable pipeline restart2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisNEWSAdam Schiff, local representatives condemn Sable pipeline restartWynne Bendell

The Environmental Defense Center hosted a press conference on May 7 with California Senator Adam Schiff and local stakeholders who condemned Sable Offshore’s oil pipeline restart. Schiff emphasized the importance of moving from fossil fuels to renewable energy sources, stating that it would be an overall positive shift. Anusha Singh / Daily Nexus In March, the Texas-based...

The Environmental Defense Center hosted a press conference on May 7 with California Senator Adam Schiff and local stakeholders who condemned Sable Offshore’s oil pipeline restart. Schiff emphasized the importance of moving from fossil fuels to renewable en...

The Environmental Defense Center hosted a press conference on May 7 with California Senator Adam Schiff and local stakeholders who condemned Sable Offshore’s oil pipeline restart. Schiff emphasized the importance of moving from fossil fuels to renewable energy sources, stating that it would be an overall positive shift. Anusha Singh / Daily Nexus In March, the Texas-based oil and gas company restarted oil production from the Santa Ynez Unit after 11 years of inactivity following a 2015 rupture that spilled over 100,000 gallons of crude oil near Refugio Beach. The restart was prompted by Secretary of Energy Chris Wright who issued a statement of approval following President Donald Trump’s March 13 executive order that invoked the Defense Production Act because of the war with Iran. The restart is in violation of an injunction placed on Sable prohibiting the restart of the specific or same oil pipeline. As of May 1, California Attorney General Rob Bonta filed a motion for a preliminary injunction of the pipeline restart, which is Bonta’s second lawsuit against the Trump administration’s efforts to restart the pipeline. Overlooking the Pacific Ocean at Shoreline Park, Environmental Defense Center (EDC) Chief Counsel Linda Krop welcomed guests with a reminder of why they are still in the fight against Sable. “We are here today because our coast and the rule of law are under threat. We’re facing an outrageous and unprecedented situation with the restart of the Sable pipeline, the same pipeline that failed in 2015 and caused one of the worst oil disasters in California history,” Krop said. Krop went on to say that every day that Sable continues its oil production “increases the risk of another massive oil spill.” Before introducing the first speaker, Krop announced that the EDC and the Center for Biological Diversity and Earth Justice will be filing a motion to “intervene and join the state of California in fighting the Trump order.” Next, 24th District Congressmember, Salud Carbajal, emphasized the environmental impact of the 2015 oil spill and the 1969 spill that sparked environmental advocacy nationwide. Carbajal said that the “pro big oil Trump administration” is putting the Central Coast at risk for another spill with the restart. “I think for many of us, the fight to stop Sable can feel personal because so many of us can vividly remember what it’s like when oil companies get reckless. Who can forget 1969 Santa Barbara and 2015 Refugio?” Carbajal said. “Oil spills both left damage to our environment, our local economy. Oil slick beaches, piles of dead wildlife and a costly cleanup bill. Simply put, they were major environmental disasters for our community.” Additionally, Carbajal condemned Trump’s “reckless” proposal to open up the California Coast to oil and gas leasing for the first time in over four decades. He reminded the audience of Trump’s campaign promise to be big oil’s “best friend” if they donated to his campaign, which Carbajal said is the “one campaign promise he has actually managed to keep.” “We do not want to gamble with our environmental and [economical] well-being, and we will not sacrifice our public safety in the name of a big oil bottom line,” Carbajal said. Next, in light of the pipeline restart, Schiff emphasized the importance of moving from fossil fuels to renewable energy sources, stating that it would be an overall positive shift. He specifically called for the end of fossil fuel reliance, stating that it would prohibit the federal government from using the “oil economy” as a justification for foreign intervention. “I think moving off of fossil fuels and moving to renewable sources of energy is an environmental imperative. It is a health imperative. It’s an economic imperative. It is a national security imperative,” Schiff said. “So many of these conflicts, like this war we’re in now with Iran, end up being about the oil economy, and the more that we can do to wean ourselves off this reliance on fossil fuels, the better we are across the board.” Schiff called out the “absurdity” of Trump invoking the Defense Production Act and using war and gas prices as reasoning to restart oil production, despite gas prices continuing to rise and oil companies seeing billions in quarterly profits. “This has nothing to do with national security. It has nothing to do with your price at the pump. It only has to do with the present, keeping a promise to the big oil companies,” Schiff said. Next, California State Assemblymember Gregg Hart condemned Trump and Sable’s illegal opposition to environmental protections. “We cannot allow Sable and the Trump administration to operate as though they are above the law, particularly laws that are designed to protect [the] environment and public safety,” Hart said. Hart promised to continue fighting against Sable and the restart of the pipeline, underscoring how critical it is to protect the health of marine life and the California coast. “We have institutions in the rule of law that we’re going to hold these federal actors accountable to. Our coastline is too important and too vulnerable to the aging infrastructure that these oil industries operators operate, and we cannot allow for weakened oversight of our regulatory processes,” Hart said. “We’re going to protect our environment and preserve these resources for future generations without repeating the mistakes of the past.” Mati Waiya, chairman of the Coastal Band of the Chumash Nation, said that the restart is in direct opposition to “children’s birthright to a healthy ecosystem and a place that they can call home.” “It’s like a nightmare. It’s going to happen again, and then we’re going to look back. We have to ask ourselves, ‘How much and how long do we accept this brutality?’” Waiya said. Brady Bradshaw, representing the Center for Biological Diversity Action Fund, spoke to the importance of continuing to resist Trump, stating that giving up is not a sustainable option. “What’s happening today is Trump’s energy agenda, which can be summed up in three words: fossil fuel authoritarianism. When big oil billionaires have a president in their pocket because of donations they made, this is what happens,” Bradshaw said. “The president [is] invoking a fake national energy emergency to force an unsafe pipeline back online. When power is abused like this, the easiest thing to do is to throw up our hands and give up, but we’re fighting back because we have to draw a line in the sand.” To conclude the event, Schiff called out Sable for its “hostile” environmental policy and announced the pipeline restart on the 10-year anniversary of the Refugio spill. “Sable was just too happy to announce the restart of this project on the 10-year anniversary of the oil spill. Who does that? Who thinks that’s a date to be celebrated and commemorated? It’s just a deliberate poke in the eye. It’s an attitude that we see frankly, throughout the administration, that says we not only don’t care about the environment, we are just affirmatively hostile, and we are proud of it,” Schiff said. A version of this article appeared on p. 1 of the May 14 print edition of the Daily Nexus. Wynne Bendell Wynne Bendell (she/her) is the University News Editor for the 2025-2026 school year. Previously, Bendell was an Assistant News Editor and a News Intern for the 2024-2025 school year. She can be reached at wynnebendell@dailynexus.com or news@dailynexus.com

value.Title: Ashtrom breaks ground on 150-MW CSP-contracted solar project
value.Overview: SeeNews Renewables, 196words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK2-DGP3-S0NJ-P43M-00000-00Ashtrom breaks ground on 150-MW CSP-contracted solar project2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisAshtrom breaks ground on 150-MW CSP-contracted solar projectSeeNews

May 14 (Renewables Now) - Israel's Ashtrom Renewable Energy has officially launched the construction of its 150-MW/195-MWdc El Patrimonio solar project in Texas, expecting the plant to become operational in 2027.

The milestone was marked by an official ceremony at the site in Bexar County, the subsidiary of Israeli construction and real estate developer Ashtrom Group Ltd (TLV:ASHG) said on Wednesday.

Being installed near San Antonio, the photovoltaic (PV) park will be operated under a 20-year power purchase agreement (PPA) with municipally-owned electric and gas utility CPS Energy. The deal will see the utility offtake around 70% of the plant's output and the associated renewable energy credits. The electricity volumes not procured by CPS Energy will be offered in the Texas open electricity market by Ashtrom.

Once online, the PV farm will be capable of generating electricity that will be equal to the power demand of around 37,000 homes per year, while offsetting some 193 tonnes of carbon dioxide emissions.

Ashtrom Renewable reached financial close on the El Patrimonio project in March. SOLV Energy is the engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) contractor.

value.Title: Clifford Chance Gains Winston & Strawn , O'Melveny Litigators
value.Overview: Clifford Chance LLP has strengthened its litigation and arbitration offerings in Houston with a former Winston & Strawn LLP partner who will serve as global head of infrastructure disputes, and a former O'Melveny & Myers LLP attorney who advises infrastructure, energy and construction companies. J. Laurens Wilkes brings more than two decades of...

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH7-0X73-S72H-22HY-00000-00Clifford Chance Gains Winston & Strawn, O'Melveny Litigators2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisNEWSClifford Chance Gains Winston & Strawn, O'Melveny LitigatorsLynn LaRowe

Clifford Chance LLP has strengthened its litigation and arbitration offerings in Houston with a former Winston & Strawn LLP partner who will serve as global head of infrastructure disputes, and a former O'Melveny & Myers LLP attorney who advises infrastructure, energy and construction companies. J. Laurens Wilkes brings more than two decades of...

Clifford Chance LLP has strengthened its litigation and arbitration offerings in Houston with a former Winston & Strawn LLP partner who will serve as global head of infrastructure disputes, and a former O'Melveny & Myers LLP attorney who advises infrastruc...

Clifford Chance LLP has strengthened its litigation and arbitration offerings in Houston with a former Winston & Strawn LLP partner who will serve as global head of infrastructure disputes, and a former O'Melveny & Myers LLP attorney who advises infrastructure, energy and construction companies. J. Laurens Wilkes brings more than two decades of experience advising clients in the U.S. and internationally across all stages of infrastructure, construction and energy projects to his new leadership role at Clifford Chance, the firm said in a statement on Monday. Wilkes said in a statement, "Clifford Chance has an exceptional global presence in infrastructure, energy, construction and projects, spanning investments and financings, project development and disputes." "I look forward to joining this talented, collaborative team to support clients across all aspects of the project lifecycle," Wilkes said. Wilkes handles front-end bid and procurement disputes, breach of contract matters, administrative actions and matters touching on product liability, fraud and fiduciary duty, trade secrets, restructuring and torts, the firm said. He routinely appears in state and federal courts and handles domestic and international arbitration for clients. Caitlin Gernert brings her practice representing clients across the energy industry, including oil and gas producers, midstream operators, power generators, liquefied natural gas developers, renewable energy companies and wastewater disposal companies, according to her firm bio. Robert Houck, Clifford Chance's global head of disputes, said in a statement, "I'm thrilled to welcome Laurens and Caitlin to the firm." "Their strong track record in guiding clients through complex issues in the energy and infrastructure sectors adds significant depth to our U.S. and global disputes team," Houck said. "This is particularly valuable as we continue to see strong demand for risk mitigation and disputes advice from clients operating in these sectors." Wilkes practiced with Winston & Strawn for a little over two years and had practiced with Jones Day for more than 16 years before that, his LinkedIn profile shows. He began his legal career with a two-year judicial clerkship in the Eastern District of Texas. Wilkes said on LinkedIn on Monday that he is "thrilled to join the team at Clifford Chance and very much looking forward to helping to grow the infrastructure disputes practice in Texas, across the U.S., and throughout the world." Wilkes earned his law degree from the University of Texas School of Law and holds a bachelor's degree from Tufts University. Alex Wilde, managing partner of Clifford Chance's Houston shop, said in a statement, "We are excited to welcome Laurens and Caitlin to the firm and Houston office." "Their significant sector experience builds on the strong momentum of our Houston office and will be instrumental in further expanding our capabilities, while also strengthening our global platform," Wilde said. Gernert practiced with O'Melveny for about a year and had practiced with Winston & Strawn for more than four years before that, her LinkedIn profile shows. Earlier in her legal career, Gernert practiced with Thompson & Knight LLP and served as a judicial law clerk in the Middle District of Pennsylvania. Gernert earned her law degree from Tulane University Law School and holds a bachelor's degree from Southern Methodist University. --Editing by Nicole Bleier.

urn:contentItem:6JG9-TCD3-RV01-S2YN-00000-00Energea Finances Start of Construction at 140 MW Iron Spur Solar Project in Texas2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:19ZLexisNexisNEWSEnergea Finances Start of Construction at 140 MW Iron Spur Solar Project in Texas

- Energea, a global renewable energy developer and operator that manages four renewable energy investment portfolios, announced that construction has begun on Iron Spur Solar, a 140 MW DC utility-scale solar power project in Snyder, Texas, for which Energea has provided financing. Construction is expected to reach full operation in early 2029. Iron Spur's...

- Energea, a global renewable energy developer and operator that manages four renewable energy investment portfolios, announced that construction has begun on Iron Spur Solar, a 140 MW DC utility-scale solar power project in Snyder, Texas, for which Energe...

- Energea, a global renewable energy developer and operator that manages four renewable energy investment portfolios, announced that construction has begun on Iron Spur Solar, a 140 MW DC utility-scale solar power project in Snyder, Texas, for which Energea has provided financing. Construction is expected to reach full operation in early 2029. Iron Spur's has secured an exclusivity agreement with an investment-grade corporate energy user for a long-term Power Purchase Agreement. This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260501012610/en/ Energea will provide up to $5 million of secured convertible financing for the Iron Spur project. An initial $762,000 initial investment was made through its Solar in the USA Portfolio. The financing is Energea’s first for an industrial-scale solar project in the U.S., utilizing an innovative convertible loan structure that generates immediate income while maintaining a clear path to majority project ownership. “Iron Spur is a strategic evolution for our Solar in the USA portfolio, adding utility-scale exposure and immediate cash generation during the traditionally non-income-producing construction phase,” said Mike Silvestrini, Co-Founder and Managing Partner at Energea. “We see excellent investment opportunities in industrial-scale solar and anticipate expanding further beyond distributed generation projects for which we are known, while maintaining Energea's focus on immediate cash yield and strong contractual protection. The Iron Spur Project speaks to our focus on risk-adjusted returns, providing secured debt protection today while preserving the optionality to convert to majority equity ownership as the project reaches key milestones.” The Iron Spur project is strategically located in West Texas, one of the most established and liquid utility-scale solar markets in the United States. The ground-mounted, single-axis tracked facility is expected to generate approximately 317 GWh of electricity annually once operational, supported by a 35-year land lease and a de-risked interconnection strategy that avoids costly substation upgrades. Rather than acquiring the project outright, Energea is providing development capital through a secured, convertible loan to the project's special purpose entity, CT Solar One, LLC. The structure features monthly cash interest payments providing immediate income, a five-year maturity on each loan advance, and a first-priority security interest in 100% of the project's equity. Iron Spur has secured key development milestones including site control, interconnection applications, and an exclusivity agreement with an investment grade buyer for a long-term Power Purchase Agreement. The project is eligible for the 30% federal Investment Tax Credit with potential domestic content upside, and benefits from a structured EPC procurement process aligned with ERCOT utility-scale benchmarks. The Iron Spur project is being developed by Levona Renewables, a U.S.-based utility-scale solar developer, and is set to be part of a broader platform, with Levona and Energea exploring additional opportunities to collaborate on future utility-scale solar projects in the region. About Energea Energea is a U.S.-based renewable energy investment platform that connects individual investors with solar projects worldwide. Energea manages four investment portfolios consisting of solar energy projects, including Solar in the USA, LATAM Energy, Solarize Africa, and Community Solar in Brazil. Since launching in 2020, Energea has raised over $450 million and generated a 12% realized IRR for investors. By combining financial returns with measurable environmental and social impact, Energea enables people to participate in the global energy transition. Learn more at energea.com.

(VAN) Prices at the pump have leapt since the start of the conflict – but clinging to fossil fuels will only prolong the pain. Customers filling up their tanks at a Shell petrol station in Hanover, April 2026. Photograph: Odd Andersen/AFP/. The car is perhaps the closest thing Germany has to a national symbol. (VAN) Prices at the pump have leapt since the start of the conflict – but clinging to fossil fuels will only prolong the pain. Customers filling up their tanks at a Shell petrol station in Hanover, April 2026. Photograph: Odd Andersen/AFP/. The car is perh...

(VAN) Prices at the pump have leapt since the start of the conflict – but clinging to fossil fuels will only prolong the pain. Customers filling up their tanks at a Shell petrol station in Hanover, April 2026. Photograph: Odd Andersen/AFP/. The car is perhaps the closest thing Germany has to a national symbol. For this reason, the success of the auto industry and the happiness of motorists has long been a barometer for the standing of the Federal Republic. Since the beginning of the war on Iran, German news has been filled with stories about drivers. Journalists have filed breathless dispatches from petrol stations all over the country, reporting scenes of anger and frustration at the hike in fuel prices. The anger is understandable. Diesel rose briefly to over €2.40 (£2.08) a litre, a more than 50% increase on the price a year ago. Because the effects of the war have been quickly felt in the form of inflation, the crisis in the strait of Hormuz has revealed just how fragile the European economy is – although it is not the first time Europeans have been forced to learn the lesson of their energy dependency. Between 2020 and 2024, Covid, the obstruction of the Suez canal by the Ever Given container ship, Russia’s war on Ukraine and Israel’s war on Gaza all disrupted global trade and EU energy security. These earlier crises should have taught the German government about how to respond politically to such shocks. But its reaction to the US-Israel war on Iran has again exposed the hypocrisy of energy politics in Germany. Friedrich Merz’s governing coalition of the Christian Democratic Union (CDU), Christian Social Union (CSU) and Social Democratic (SPD) parties, has responded to the latest disruption to oil shipments by doubling down on its commitment to fossil fuels. This has taken the form of new subsidies for fossil fuels and drafting new laws that could defund renewable energy projects. On 23 March, Katherina Reiche, Germany’s minister for economic affairs and energy, gave a remarkable speech at an energy conference in Houston, Texas, in which she called into question the EU law which enshrines the goal of net zero emissions by 2050. “There is a flexibility we have to get back,” she said, adding that this could only be achieved by “allowing different solutions and technologies” and accepting that it could mean the EU may miss its net zero goal by “maybe 5 or 10% by 2050”. This apparent reversal from renewable energy was not inevitable. Soon after the war on Iran started, the European Commission president, Ursula von der Leyen (herself a member of the CDU and a longstanding minister under Angela Merkel), made the case for a green transition that could not have been further from Reiche’s statement two weeks later. “Ten days of war have already cost European taxpayers an additional €3bn in fossil fuel imports,” von der Leyen told the European parliament in Strasbourg. “That is the price of our dependence. The fact is, we have energy sources that are homegrown: renewables and nuclear. Their prices have remained the same over the last 10 days.” At the root of this disconnect between Brussels and Berlin is the CDU and CSU’s systematic disregard for combatting the climate crisis, articulated most vociferously by Reiche. Prior to taking up her ministerial post, Reiche was the CEO of Westenergie AG, a subsidiary of the energy company E.ON. Her background has drawn harsh criticism from those who see the energy minister as too close to the fossil fuel industry, which has its own set of interests. She seemingly proved her critics right this month, when she opposed a plan put forward by the SPD to tax the excess profits of petroleum companies. Environmental groups became even more alarmed when Reiche announced she would be halting construction of wind and solar farms and cutting programmes subsidising private solar modules. In their place she has proposed erecting new gas plants. In November, Reiche justified her policy mission in the name of efficiency. “Subsidies and public funding programmes have to be in turn rigorously reviewed,” she said. “Wrong incentives have to be dismantled, even if it hurts.” She hinted at cuts to subsidies for installing heat pumps, which had been introduced by her predecessor from the Green party. Before the Iran war, Reiche promised she would let the market dictate policy, not the other way around. But she has been happy to carve out an exception for the fossil fuel industry. For instance, she wanted to increase subsidies that would disproportionately benefit motorists who need their car to commute to work. Ultimately, the government agreed to introduce a similarly misdirected subsidy: a tax cut for fuel sold at petrol stations. This will be costly and effectively amount to a transfer of state funds to companies, harming Germans who do not need to fill up the tanks of their cars. The current crisis – the biggest oil shock in decades – has shown that fossil fuels are neither economically nor environmentally sustainable. The subsidies should therefore go towards extending renewable energy. But the CDU-led government is doing the exact opposite. The war has proven that when the interests of motorists are at stake in Germany, free market ideology goes out of the window. In late March, a law restricting petrol stations to no more than one price rise a day was swiftly drafted and passed. In an ideal world, the government would extend the sympathy it offers to motorists by widening the net of relief packages. But when it comes to who is worth spending money on, hypocrisy seems to rule the day.
